caption:
Subscribe to support the 25th anniversary! 

#wtc #911 #truth #neverforget #usa
audio_track_actual: Single narrator delivering an introductory-physics walk-through of the WTC collapse over diagrams and time-elapsed collapse footage.
video_overlay_text_observed: Schematic diagrams of the towers with vector arrows demonstrating expected vs observed collapse behavior; on-screen time stamps comparing observed collapse times (10–12 s) against the calculated absolute free-fall reference (9.2 s).
audio_content_summary: The narrator argues that the World Trade Center towers fell at near-free-fall acceleration — observed at 10–12 seconds versus a 9.2-second absolute free-fall reference — and that this is inconsistent with conservation of momentum and conservation of energy in the official fire-and-impact collapse model. The argument relies on a basic physics principle: that energy used to break apart the lower structure must come from the kinetic energy of the falling upper section, and the falling section therefore cannot maintain free-fall speed while simultaneously destroying obstacles in its path. The narrator concludes that an additional energy input — implicitly, explosives — must be present for the observed behavior to occur. NIST has acknowledged a 2.25-second pure-free-fall period for WTC 7 in its NCSTAR 1A final report; its position is that the energy released by the upper-section impact so exceeded the lower structure's resistance that the observed acceleration is reconcilable with conventional physics (see 2001-wtc-collapse-physics-001 for the detailed comparison).
implied_frame: The reel frames the physics-based critique as definitionally settled — 'high school physics' — and presents NIST's position as a violation of established law. NIST's published responses (in its FAQs and the Europhysics News peer-reviewed treatment) engage the same physics with different boundary-condition assumptions. The reel's argument is the most-cited public-facing form of the AE911Truth structural-engineering critique.
capture_method: Headless browser recording of the in-page <video> element via MediaRecorder over captureStream(), viewport 1280x800.
